description: Vitis labrusca, the fox grape, is a species of grapevines belonging to the Vitis genus in the flowering plant family Vitaceae. The vines are native to eastern North America and are the source of many grape cultivars, including Catawba, Concord, Delaware, Isabella, Niagara, and many hybrid grape varieties such as Agawam, Alexander and Onaka.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.edible_parts: The sweet to tart berries are edible.

description: Vitis mustangensis, commonly known as the mustang grape, is a species of grape that is native to the southern United States. Its range includes parts of Mississippi, Alabama, Louisiana, Texas, and Oklahoma.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.edible_parts: The sweet to tart berries are edible.

description: The immature fruits are small green berries. As they mature, the berries become bluish-black with a white bloom and measure 1/4 to 1/2- inch in diameter. They appear in clusters and mature in late summer and fall. They contain 1 to 4 flat, egg-shaped seeds.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.edible_parts: The berries are sour until the first frost then they become sweeter. They may be eaten fresh, dried, or made into jellies or wine. It is important to properly identify the berries before ingestion.

description: The vine produces 1/2 to 1 inch, dark purple or bronze, thick-skinned berries. In North Carolina, the grapes are primarily purple in color and are available from August to October.

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
common_problems: Diseases may include powdery mildew, black rot, bitter rot, and leaf spot.
Their resistance to Pierce's disease and the insect phylloxera makes this vine valuable as a commercial crop.
common_pests: Their resistance to Pierce's disease and the insect phylloxera makes this vine valuable as a commercial crop.
These pests are widespread in the Southeast United States, killing the plant's roots.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) Native Americans used this fruit for food, medicine, and in some ceremonies.
care.edible_parts: Fruit can be eaten fresh or is used to make wine and an assortment of jams, jellies, and other preserves.

description: Vitis vinifera, the common grape vine, is a species of flowering plant, native to the Mediterranean region, Central Europe, and southwestern Asia, from Morocco and Portugal north to southern Germany and east to northern Iran. As of 2012, there were between 5,000 and 10,000 varieties of Vitis vinifera grapes though only a few are of commercial significance for wine and table grape production.
origin: Mediterranean, Central Europe and southwestern Asia

care.light_guide: NC State: Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)
care.tips: Good companions: Common bean, Tansy, Tomato, White Mustard, Yarrow | Keep apart: Garlic, Raphanus sativus | Similar plants: Carya illinoinensis
common_problems: This plant is particularly susceptible to grape phylloxera.
Diseases include gray mold, crown gall, botrytis bunch rot, black rot, downy mildew and powdery mildew.
common_pests: This insect damages roots and disrupts water and nutrient intake which results in loss of leaf surface area, yield reduction and total demise of the plant over a period of 4 to 7 years.
Other insect problems can include mealybugs, flea beetles, leaf hopper, leaf roller, Japanese beetle and grape berry moth.
care.used_for_details: (NC State ethnobotany) Used in food and winemaking

description: In North Carolina, the round fruits are available from August to November.
